rbc.name: Red Blood Cell Count
rbc.mito_mechanism: Tracks oxygen delivery and hypoxia-linked mitochondrial adaptation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Strong hypoxic signaling and blood-viscosity stress may be present.

high.mito_impact: High RBC suggests chronic hypoxic drive and compensatory mitochondrial stress.

optimal.mito_impact: Oxygen carrying capacity is compatible with efficient oxidative metabolism.

low.mito_impact: Low RBC can limit oxygen delivery and reduce ETC throughput.

wbc.name: White Blood Cell Count
wbc.mito_mechanism: Reflects inflammatory tone, mtROS, and innate immune mitochondrial signaling.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked leukocytosis suggests strong mtROS-driven inflammation.

high.mito_impact: Elevated WBC fits inflammatory mitochondrial stress.

optimal.mito_impact: Immune tone appears compatible with balanced mitochondrial signaling.

low.mito_impact: Low WBC may reflect low nutrient reserve or impaired immune-cell energetics.

hgb.name: Hemoglobin
hgb.mito_mechanism: Captures heme synthesis, oxygen transport, and mitochondrial respiratory support.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very high hemoglobin can signal hypoxic compensation and delivery mismatch.

high.mito_impact: High hemoglobin suggests persistent hypoxic signaling.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports efficient oxygen transport for oxidative phosphorylation.

low.mito_impact: Low hemoglobin reduces oxygen delivery and exercise tolerance.

hct.name: Hematocrit
hct.mito_mechanism: Integrates oxygen carrying capacity, plasma volume, and hypoxia stress.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high hematocrit raises perfusion stress and oxygen extraction mismatch.

high.mito_impact: High hematocrit fits chronic hypoxic compensation.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports stable oxygen delivery for aerobic metabolism.

low.mito_impact: Low hematocrit can constrain oxidative metabolism.

mcv.name: Mean Corpuscular Volume
mcv.mito_mechanism: Links one-carbon metabolism and mitochondrial nutrient support to erythrocyte maturation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked macrocytosis suggests impaired methylation and mitochondrial nutrient flux.

high.mito_impact: High MCV often reflects suboptimal one-carbon support.

optimal.mito_impact: Consistent with balanced erythrocyte maturation.

low.mito_impact: Low MCV often signals iron-restricted heme synthesis.

mch.name: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin
mch.mito_mechanism: Reflects hemoglobin loading and mitochondrial support of heme synthesis.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very high MCH usually follows macrocytosis and impaired nutrient flux.

high.mito_impact: High MCH can accompany macrocytosis and altered mitochondrial support.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with adequate heme incorporation.

low.mito_impact: Low MCH suggests iron-restricted oxygen carrying capacity.

mchc.name: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration
mchc.mito_mechanism: Links iron availability, oxidative stress, and erythrocyte membrane stability.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high MCHC can reflect membrane oxidative injury.

high.mito_impact: High MCHC may indicate membrane stress and oxidation.

optimal.mito_impact: Consistent with stable erythrocyte membrane function.

low.mito_impact: Low MCHC often signals iron-restricted heme synthesis.

plt.name: Platelet Count
plt.mito_mechanism: Platelet production and activation are tightly linked to mitochondrial membrane potential.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked thrombocytosis can accompany strong inflammatory mitochondrial signaling.

high.mito_impact: High platelets fit inflammatory stress and platelet mitochondrial activation.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable membrane function and low inflammatory burden.

low.mito_impact: Low platelets can reflect impaired marrow energetics or excessive consumption.

neutrophils.name: Neutrophils
neutrophils.mito_mechanism: Neutrophil activation depends on glycolytic switching, mtROS, and innate immune stress.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked neutrophilia suggests strong innate immune activation and mtROS load.

high.mito_impact: High neutrophils indicate inflammatory mitochondrial stress.

optimal.mito_impact: Innate immune activity appears balanced.

low.mito_impact: Low neutrophils may reflect reduced immune-cell production and nutrient reserve.

lymphocytes.name: Lymphocytes
lymphocytes.mito_mechanism: Lymphocyte fitness depends on mitochondrial remodeling, redox control, and NAD+ status.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked lymphocytosis may reflect persistent adaptive immune activation.

high.mito_impact: High lymphocytes suggest increased immune-cell mitochondrial demand.

optimal.mito_impact: Adaptive immune support appears balanced.

low.mito_impact: Low lymphocytes can accompany chronic stress and low NAD+ reserve.

lh.name: Luteinizing Hormone
lh.mito_mechanism: Reflects pituitary-gonadal energetics and steroidogenic mitochondrial function.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked LH elevation can signal compensatory reproductive stress.

high.mito_impact: High LH fits compensatory drive when steroidogenesis is inefficient.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable reproductive mitochondrial signaling.

low.mito_impact: Low LH may reflect energy-conservation signaling.

fsh.name: Follicle-Stimulating Hormone
fsh.mito_mechanism: Tracks reproductive-axis energy status and mitochondrial protein import capacity.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked FSH elevation can reflect compensatory reproductive stress.

high.mito_impact: High FSH fits suboptimal reproductive mitochondrial support.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced reproductive signaling.

low.mito_impact: Low FSH can reflect hypothalamic-pituitary downshifting during low energy availability.

testosterone.name: Testosterone
testosterone.mito_mechanism: Depends on mitochondrial cholesterol transport and steroidogenic electron transfer.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked testosterone excess can reflect dysregulated steroidogenic signaling.

high.mito_impact: High testosterone can increase anabolic mitochondrial demand.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports mitochondrial biogenesis, muscle maintenance, and redox resilience.

low.mito_impact: Low testosterone is linked to reduced mitochondrial biogenesis and poorer muscle energetics.

estradiol.name: Estradiol
estradiol.mito_mechanism: Regulates antioxidant enzymes, membrane architecture, and mitochondrial substrate use.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked estradiol excess can shift redox signaling and membrane lipid balance.

high.mito_impact: High estradiol may alter membrane signaling and inflammatory tone.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports antioxidant defenses and mitochondrial membrane resilience.

low.mito_impact: Low estradiol can weaken antioxidant tone and membrane dynamics.

prolactin.name: Prolactin
prolactin.mito_mechanism: Sensitive to stress signaling, dopaminergic tone, and hypothalamic energy status.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hyperprolactinemia can signal strong stress-axis disruption.

high.mito_impact: High prolactin fits chronic stress signaling and mitochondrial energy conservation.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced stress-axis and reproductive signaling.

low.mito_impact: Low prolactin is usually not a major mitochondrial concern in isolation.

shbg.name: Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin
shbg.mito_mechanism: Tracks hepatic metabolic tone, insulin sensitivity, and NAD+-linked regulation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high SHBG can accompany low energy availability and low anabolic signaling.

high.mito_impact: High SHBG often aligns with a catabolic metabolic state.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced hepatic metabolism and hormone transport.

low.mito_impact: Low SHBG frequently indicates insulin resistance and mTOR-dominant nutrient signaling.

psa.name: Prostate-Specific Antigen
psa.mito_mechanism: A tissue-stress marker influenced by inflammatory and proliferative mitochondrial signaling.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked PSA elevation suggests significant tissue stress and possible proliferative rewiring.

high.mito_impact: High PSA may reflect inflammatory or proliferative pressure with increased mitochondrial demand.

optimal.mito_impact: Does not suggest meaningful prostate inflammatory burden.

low.mito_impact: Low PSA does not indicate a mitochondrial concern.

glucose.name: Glucose
glucose.mito_mechanism: Reflects mitochondrial substrate selection, pyruvate handling, and AMPK-mTOR balance.

critically_high.mito_impact: Severe hyperglycemia drives mtROS and metabolic inflexibility.

high.mito_impact: High glucose indicates pressure on PDC regulation and oxidative metabolism.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with flexible substrate use and stable fuel handling.

low.mito_impact: Low glucose may reflect inadequate intake or counterregulatory stress.

insulin.name: Insulin
insulin.mito_mechanism: Integrates nutrient sensing, AKT-mTOR signaling, and mitochondrial fuel switching.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hyperinsulinemia indicates strong nutrient-surplus signaling.

high.mito_impact: High insulin suggests reduced insulin sensitivity and lower oxidative flexibility.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with efficient substrate partitioning.

low.mito_impact: Low insulin may accompany low intake or pancreatic reserve issues.

homa_ir.name: HOMA-IR
homa_ir.mito_mechanism: Summarizes fasting glucose-insulin coupling and mitochondrial substrate inflexibility.

critically_high.mito_impact: Severely elevated HOMA-IR strongly links to impaired mitochondrial fuel selection.

high.mito_impact: High HOMA-IR reflects insulin-resistant signaling and reduced oxidative efficiency.

optimal.mito_impact: Insulin sensitivity appears compatible with flexible mitochondrial fuel handling.

low.mito_impact: Low HOMA-IR usually does not suggest mitochondrial stress in isolation.

ast.name: AST
ast.mito_mechanism: Partly mitochondrial in origin and rises with hepatocellular or myocellular mitochondrial stress.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked AST elevation suggests substantial mitochondrial membrane injury.

high.mito_impact: High AST fits mitochondrial stress signaling and mitokine release.

optimal.mito_impact: AST is aligned with low hepatocellular mitochondrial strain.

low.mito_impact: Low AST is usually not a primary mitochondrial concern.

alt.name: ALT
alt.mito_mechanism: Reflects hepatic metabolic overload and mitochondrial substrate congestion.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked ALT elevation suggests significant hepatocellular stress and impaired metabolic handling.

high.mito_impact: High ALT fits hepatic mitochondrial overload and reduced beta-oxidation efficiency.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with manageable hepatic metabolic load.

low.mito_impact: Low ALT is usually not a mitochondrial concern in isolation.

ggtp.name: GGTP
ggtp.mito_mechanism: Tracks glutathione turnover and mitochondrial redox burden.

critically_high.mito_impact: Severely elevated GGTP suggests strong oxidative stress and glutathione cycling demand.

high.mito_impact: High GGTP indicates increased glutathione turnover and oxidative pressure.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced glutathione handling.

low.mito_impact: Low GGTP is typically not concerning from a mitochondrial perspective.

de_ritis_ratio.name: De Ritis Ratio
de_ritis_ratio.mito_mechanism: A high AST to ALT ratio can point to a stronger mitochondrial contribution to enzyme leakage.

critically_high.mito_impact: A very high ratio can reflect substantial mitochondrial injury or catabolic tissue stress.

high.mito_impact: A high ratio is compatible with mitochondrial membrane stress.

optimal.mito_impact: The AST to ALT balance does not suggest disproportionate mitochondrial leakage.

low.mito_impact: A low ratio usually points more toward cytosolic than mitochondrial liver stress.

urea.name: Urea
urea.mito_mechanism: Reflects protein turnover, renal clearance, and mitochondrial nitrogen disposal.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high urea can reflect dehydration, catabolism, or reduced renal energetic reserve.

high.mito_impact: High urea suggests higher catabolic load and increased nitrogen-handling demand.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced protein turnover and renal support.

low.mito_impact: Low urea may reflect low protein intake or reduced hepatic urea-cycle throughput.

creatinine.name: Creatinine
creatinine.mito_mechanism: Reflects muscle energy turnover and renal clearance, both shaped by mitochondrial function.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high creatinine suggests reduced renal reserve or excessive tissue breakdown.

high.mito_impact: High creatinine can indicate lower renal energetic reserve or increased muscle turnover.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable muscle turnover and renal clearance.

low.mito_impact: Low creatinine may reflect low muscle mass and lower mitochondrial buffering capacity.

uric_acid.name: Uric Acid
uric_acid.mito_mechanism: Has a U-shaped relationship with mitochondrial health through antioxidant buffering and ATP degradation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hyperuricemia points to excess purine turnover and oxidative stress.

high.mito_impact: High uric acid often signals oxidative stress and accelerated ATP degradation.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced redox buffering and purine turnover.

low.mito_impact: Very low uric acid can imply reduced antioxidant buffering capacity.

total_cholesterol.name: Total Cholesterol
total_cholesterol.mito_mechanism: Shapes steroidogenesis, membrane architecture, and mitochondrial contact-site biology.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hypercholesterolemia may reflect impaired lipid oxidation and membrane stress.

high.mito_impact: High cholesterol can signal reduced lipid clearance and altered membrane composition.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable membrane and steroidogenic function.

low.mito_impact: Low cholesterol can accompany low steroidogenic substrate availability.

ldl.name: LDL Cholesterol
ldl.mito_mechanism: Rises with altered hepatic export, redox imbalance, and impaired lipid oxidation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very high LDL increases oxidative membrane stress and ferroptosis-prone signaling.

high.mito_impact: High LDL suggests oxidative lipid handling pressure.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with manageable lipid trafficking and redox load.

low.mito_impact: Low LDL usually is not a mitochondrial concern in isolation.

hdl.name: HDL Cholesterol
hdl.mito_mechanism: Tracks reverse cholesterol transport and metabolic flexibility linked to AMPK-SIRT signaling.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very high HDL can occur with altered lipid remodeling, but context matters.

high.mito_impact: Higher HDL is often compatible with favorable lipid handling.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports efficient lipid trafficking and membrane maintenance.

low.mito_impact: Low HDL suggests weaker lipid clearance and lower metabolic flexibility.

triglycerides.name: Triglycerides
triglycerides.mito_mechanism: Increase when beta-oxidation and hepatic export are mismatched to nutrient flux.

critically_high.mito_impact: Severely elevated triglycerides signal metabolic inflexibility and impaired lipid oxidation.

high.mito_impact: High triglycerides suggest reduced fatty-acid oxidation and mTOR-dominant nutrient signaling.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable hepatic lipid handling and substrate switching.

low.mito_impact: Low triglycerides can accompany efficient fatty-acid oxidation or low intake.

non_hdl.name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
non_hdl.mito_mechanism: Summarizes atherogenic lipid cargo and often rises with impaired lipid oxidation.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very high non-HDL indicates substantial atherogenic lipid stress and membrane oxidation.

high.mito_impact: High non-HDL suggests inefficient lipid processing and increased oxidative stress.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with manageable lipid cargo and redox stress.

low.mito_impact: Low non-HDL does not suggest a mitochondrial concern in isolation.

urine_ph.name: Urine pH
urine_ph.mito_mechanism: Reflects acid-base handling, mitochondrial substrate oxidation, and renal buffering.

critically_high.mito_impact: Very alkaline urine can reflect altered renal acid handling or infection.

high.mito_impact: Higher urine pH may indicate lower acid load or altered buffering dynamics.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced renal acid-base support.

low.mito_impact: Acidic urine can reflect higher acid load, ketosis, or altered substrate use.

urine_protein.name: Urine Protein
urine_protein.mito_mechanism: Can reflect renal energetic stress, glomerular barrier strain, and inflammatory mitochondrial signaling.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked proteinuria suggests substantial renal stress that may include mitochondrial dysfunction.

high.mito_impact: Elevated urine protein is compatible with renal energetic strain and inflammation.

optimal.mito_impact: Does not suggest meaningful renal mitochondrial stress.

low.mito_impact: Low urine protein is expected and not concerning.

urine_glucose.name: Urine Glucose
urine_glucose.mito_mechanism: Signals glycemic spillover and systemic substrate overload that stresses mitochondria.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked glycosuria indicates severe glucose handling impairment and metabolic stress.

high.mito_impact: Urine glucose suggests substrate overload and reduced mitochondrial flexibility.

optimal.mito_impact: No glycosuria is consistent with preserved glucose handling.

low.mito_impact: Zero urine glucose is expected and not concerning.

urine_ketones.name: Urine Ketones
urine_ketones.mito_mechanism: Reflects fat oxidation and hepatic mitochondrial ketogenesis with context-dependent meaning.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked ketonuria can indicate severe carbohydrate deficit or dysglycemia.

high.mito_impact: Elevated ketones suggest a strong shift toward fatty-acid oxidation.

optimal.mito_impact: Minimal ketones are compatible with mixed-fuel metabolism.

low.mito_impact: Absent ketones are expected in a mixed-fed state.

vitamin_d.name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
vitamin_d.mito_mechanism: Influences oxidative stress defense, calcium handling, and repair signaling.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high vitamin D can disrupt calcium handling and membrane stability.

high.mito_impact: Higher vitamin D may be acceptable, but excess can shift calcium flux and redox balance.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports antioxidant defenses and mitochondrial resilience.

low.mito_impact: Low vitamin D can weaken antioxidant signaling and membrane stability.

sodium.name: Sodium
sodium.mito_mechanism: Shapes cellular excitability, volume regulation, and ATP cost of membrane transport.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hypernatremia increases osmotic stress and transport energy demand.

high.mito_impact: High sodium suggests dehydration or osmotic stress.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with stable membrane transport and energetic homeostasis.

low.mito_impact: Low sodium can impair excitability and raise stress signaling around ion gradients.

potassium.name: Potassium
potassium.mito_mechanism: Influences membrane potential, calcium handling, and ATP cost of excitability.

critically_high.mito_impact: Marked hyperkalemia can destabilize membrane potential and bioenergetics.

high.mito_impact: High potassium may alter membrane polarization and calcium handling.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports stable membrane potential and mitochondrial-calcium coupling.

low.mito_impact: Low potassium can impair membrane potential stability and muscular energetics.

ft3.name: Free T3
ft3.mito_mechanism: A master regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis, oxygen consumption, and protein import.

critically_high.mito_impact: Markedly high FT3 can increase oxygen demand and redox stress beyond recovery capacity.

high.mito_impact: High FT3 raises mitochondrial throughput and oxidative load if recovery is poor.

optimal.mito_impact: Supports mitochondrial biogenesis and efficient energy production.

low.mito_impact: Low FT3 is associated with downregulated mitochondrial biogenesis and oxidative capacity.

homocysteine.name: Homocysteine
homocysteine.mito_mechanism: Directly impairs ETC Complex I-IV via oxidative damage, raises mtROS, and disrupts membrane potential.

critically_high.mito_impact: Severe Complex I inhibition, ROS overproduction, and membrane potential collapse are plausible.

high.mito_impact: Elevated homocysteine increases oxidative stress and pressures sulfur metabolism and ETC function.

optimal.mito_impact: Compatible with balanced methylation, transsulfuration, and mitochondrial redox control.

low.mito_impact: Low homocysteine is usually benign, though very low values can reflect low methionine availability.
